families.label: Common Roots
families.blurb: The one stream every branch below flows from: Israel, Jesus and the apostles, the early church, and the great councils that confessed the faith.

families.label: Church of the East
families.blurb: The ancient church of Persia and Asia, separated after the Council of Ephesus (431); its missionaries reached India and China centuries before the West did.

families.label: Oriental Orthodox
families.blurb: The churches of Egypt, Syria, Armenia, Ethiopia and India that did not accept the Council of Chalcedon (451) — among the oldest continuous churches on earth.

families.label: Eastern Orthodox
families.blurb: The Chalcedonian churches of the Greek East — Constantinople, the Slavic lands and beyond — formally separated from Rome in the Great Schism of 1054.

families.label: Catholic
families.blurb: The Western church in communion with the bishop of Rome — by far the largest single Christian body, present in nearly every nation.

families.label: Reformation Forerunners
families.blurb: Medieval movements that reached for Scripture in the people's language and a simpler church — a century and more before Luther.

families.label: Lutheran
families.blurb: The churches that grew from Martin Luther's recovery of justification by faith alone — Germany, Scandinavia, and their daughter churches worldwide.

families.label: Reformed & Presbyterian
families.blurb: The Swiss stream of the Reformation — Zwingli, Calvin, Knox — spreading through the Netherlands, Scotland, France, and the Puritans to the wider world.

families.label: Anglican
families.blurb: The Church of England and its global communion — reformed in doctrine, ancient in structure, a via media that later birthed Methodism and much else.

families.label: Anabaptists & Free Churches
families.blurb: The 'radical reformation' and the free-church dissenters: believers' baptism, gathered churches, simplicity and peace — Mennonites, Amish, Brethren, Quakers.

families.label: Baptist
families.blurb: Churches of baptized believers, congregationally governed — born among English separatists in 1609 and now one of the largest Protestant families.

families.label: Methodist & Holiness
families.blurb: The Wesleyan revival inside the Church of England became a worldwide family — and its holiness stream later kindled Pentecostalism.

families.label: Restorationist & Adventist
families.blurb: American movements that set out to restore New Testament Christianity — the Stone-Campbell churches, the Brethren, and the Adventist stream.

families.label: Pentecostal & Charismatic
families.blurb: The 20th century's great renewal of the Spirit's gifts — from Azusa Street to the charismatic movement and today's global church-planting families.

families.label: African-Initiated
families.blurb: Churches founded by African leaders on African soil — from the Zionist and Apostolic movements to some of the largest congregations in the world.

families.label: Non-Nicene Groups
families.blurb: Groups that grew out of Christian history but do not confess the Nicene faith in the Trinity — shown for the sake of the map, and badged clearly.

nodes.name: Ancient Israel
nodes.blurb: God calls Abraham and covenants with his family — the people through whom the Messiah comes and the Scriptures are given.

nodes.name: Second Temple Judaism
nodes.blurb: After the exile the Temple is rebuilt; synagogues, scribes, Pharisees and Sadducees shape the world Jesus is born into.

nodes.name: Jesus & the Apostles
nodes.blurb: The life, death and resurrection of Jesus, and the church he builds on the apostles — the trunk of everything on this page.

nodes.name: Rabbinic Judaism
nodes.blurb: After the Temple's destruction, Judaism is rebuilt around Torah, synagogue and the rabbis — Christianity's elder sibling, not its child.

nodes.name: The Early Church
nodes.blurb: From Jerusalem to the whole empire in three centuries — through persecution, the writing of the New Testament, and the church fathers.

nodes.name: Nicene Christianity
nodes.blurb: The councils of Nicaea and Constantinople confess Christ as fully God — the creed nearly every church on this page still holds.

nodes.name: The Five Patriarchates
nodes.blurb: Rome, Constantinople, Alexandria, Antioch and Jerusalem — the five great sees of the ancient church, from which the oldest branches descend.

nodes.name: Church of the East
nodes.blurb: The church of the Persian Empire, out of communion after the Council of Ephesus; often called 'Nestorian', though it rejects the label. Its missionaries reached China by 635.

nodes.name: St Thomas Christians (India)
nodes.blurb: India's ancient Christians trace their founding to the apostle Thomas; for centuries they worshipped in fellowship with the Church of the East.

nodes.name: Assyrian Church of the East
nodes.blurb: The continuing Church of the East, its patriarchate today in Erbil, Iraq — a small, ancient communion scattered by a century of persecution.

nodes.name: Ancient Church of the East
nodes.blurb: A 1968 division within the Assyrian church, largely over the calendar; based in Baghdad.

nodes.name: Oriental Orthodoxy
nodes.blurb: The churches that could not accept Chalcedon's wording about Christ's two natures — 'miaphysite', confessing one united divine-human nature.

nodes.name: Coptic Orthodox Church
nodes.blurb: Egypt's ancient church, tracing its founding to Mark the evangelist in Alexandria; the mother church of African Christianity.

nodes.name: Syriac Orthodox Church
nodes.blurb: The church of Antioch's Syriac-speaking heartland, still praying in a dialect of the language Jesus spoke.

nodes.name: Armenian Apostolic Church
nodes.blurb: Armenia became the first Christian kingdom in 301; its church later stood with the non-Chalcedonian family.

nodes.name: Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo
nodes.blurb: Founded under Frumentius and the kings of Aksum; daughter of the Coptic church until full independence in 1959, and Africa's largest ancient church.

nodes.name: Eritrean Orthodox Tewahedo
nodes.blurb: Became a self-governing church after Eritrean independence, with the Coptic pope's blessing.

nodes.name: Malankara (Indian) Orthodox
nodes.blurb: After the Coonan Cross Oath against Portuguese control, many St Thomas Christians aligned with the Syriac Orthodox — India's Oriental Orthodox family.

nodes.name: Eastern Orthodox Church
nodes.blurb: The Great Schism of 1054 formalized centuries of drift between Greek East and Latin West — over authority, culture, and a clause in the creed.

nodes.name: Ecumenical Patriarchate
nodes.blurb: Constantinople's patriarch, 'first among equals' of the Orthodox world — a seniority of honour, not a papacy.

nodes.name: Georgian Orthodox Church
nodes.blurb: Georgia's national church, evangelized by St Nino in the 320s; its independence was suppressed under Russia and restored in 1917.

nodes.name: Bulgarian Orthodox Church
nodes.blurb: The first Slavic national church to win its own patriarchate, in 927 — fruit of the mission of Cyril and Methodius's disciples.

nodes.name: Russian Orthodox Church
nodes.blurb: Prince Vladimir's baptism of Kyiv in 988 planted the faith among the Rus; Moscow became a patriarchate in 1589 — the largest Orthodox church.

nodes.name: Serbian Orthodox Church
nodes.blurb: St Sava won self-government for the Serbian church in 1219; it remains at the heart of Serbian identity.

nodes.name: Old Believers
nodes.blurb: Russians who refused Patriarch Nikon's revisions to the service books, and were fiercely persecuted for keeping the old rites.

nodes.name: Church of Greece
nodes.blurb: Independent after the Greek War of Independence — the national church of the modern Greek state.

nodes.name: Romanian Orthodox Church
nodes.blurb: United and self-governing from 1872, a patriarchate from 1925 — today the second-largest Orthodox church.

nodes.name: Orthodox Church in America
nodes.blurb: Grown from the Russian mission to Alaska (1794), granted self-rule by Moscow in 1970.

nodes.name: Orthodox Church of Ukraine
nodes.blurb: Recognized by Constantinople in 2019 as Ukraine's self-governing church — a recognition Moscow rejects; the wound is still open.

nodes.name: Roman Catholic Church
nodes.blurb: The Western church under the bishop of Rome — through the medieval centuries, the Counter-Reformation, and two Vatican councils, the largest body in Christianity.

nodes.name: Eastern Catholic Churches
nodes.blurb: Twenty-three Eastern churches in full communion with Rome while keeping their own rites — Maronite, Chaldean, Ukrainian Greek Catholic, Melkite, Syro-Malabar and more.

nodes.name: Traditionalist movements (SSPX)
nodes.blurb: After Vatican II, Archbishop Lefebvre's Society of St Pius X kept the old Latin Mass; its standing with Rome remains irregular.

nodes.name: Waldensians
nodes.blurb: Peter Waldo of Lyon gave away his wealth and sent out lay preachers with a vernacular Bible — persecuted for centuries, and still alive in Italy today.

nodes.name: Wycliffe & the Lollards
nodes.blurb: John Wycliffe's circle translated the first complete English Bible and questioned the church's wealth and hierarchy — 'the morning star of the Reformation'.

nodes.name: Jan Hus & the Hussites
nodes.blurb: The Bohemian reformer, burned at Constance in 1415; his followers defied crusades and kept a reformed church in Bohemia a century before Luther.

nodes.name: Moravian Church
nodes.blurb: The Unity of the Brethren, renewed at Herrnhut in 1727 — pioneers of Protestant world mission whose warmth helped light John Wesley's heart.

nodes.name: The Reformation
nodes.blurb: Luther's Ninety-five Theses open a recovery of the gospel — salvation by grace alone through faith alone, and the Bible in the people's tongue. Every Protestant branch below descends from here.

nodes.name: Lutheran State Churches
nodes.blurb: Germany and all Scandinavia adopt the Lutheran confession — Sweden 1527, Denmark and Norway 1536 — folk churches that carried the faith for centuries.

nodes.name: Lutheran Church–Missouri Synod
nodes.blurb: Confessional German emigrants in America, holding firmly to the Book of Concord and the inerrancy of Scripture.

nodes.name: Evangelical Lutheran Church in America
nodes.blurb: The 1988 merger of America's larger Lutheran bodies — the mainline branch of US Lutheranism.

nodes.name: Reformed Churches
nodes.blurb: Zwingli in Zurich and Calvin in Geneva build the Reformation's second great stream — God's sovereignty, covenant, and churches governed by elders.

nodes.name: French Huguenots
nodes.blurb: France's Reformed church, organized in 1559 and refined by fire — massacre and exile scattered Huguenot believers (and their skills) across the world, including the Cape.

nodes.name: Dutch Reformed Church
nodes.blurb: The Reformed church of the Netherlands, whose Synod of Dort (1618–19) gave the classic five points of Calvinism.

nodes.name: NG Kerk (Dutch Reformed, South Africa)
nodes.blurb: Planted at the Cape with Van Riebeeck's settlement (first congregation 1665) — for three centuries the largest church of the Afrikaans world.

nodes.name: Church of Scotland (Presbyterians)
nodes.blurb: John Knox brings Geneva's reformation home; Scotland's kirk, governed by elders and courts, becomes the mother of world Presbyterianism.

nodes.name: Congregationalists (Puritans)
nodes.blurb: English Puritans who gathered self-governing congregations of the converted; the Mayflower Pilgrims carried the pattern to New England in 1620.

nodes.name: American Presbyterians
nodes.blurb: The first American presbytery met in Philadelphia in 1706; today's PC(USA) descends from a long line of unions.

nodes.name: Presbyterian Church in America
nodes.blurb: A conservative continuing church formed in 1973, holding the Westminster Standards and the inerrancy of Scripture.

nodes.name: United Church of Christ
nodes.blurb: The 1957 union that gathered America's Congregational and Evangelical-and-Reformed churches into one mainline body.

nodes.name: Church of England
nodes.blurb: Henry VIII's break with Rome became, under Cranmer and Elizabeth, a reformed church with an ancient shape — Bible, Prayer Book, bishops.

nodes.name: English Separatists
nodes.blurb: Those who despaired of reforming the established church from within and left it — the seedbed of Baptists, Congregationalists and Quakers.

nodes.name: Episcopal Church (USA)
nodes.blurb: American Anglicans reorganized after independence — the first self-governing church of what became the Anglican Communion.

nodes.name: The Anglican Communion
nodes.blurb: The worldwide family of Anglican churches, meeting since the first Lambeth Conference of 1867; its centre of gravity is now the global South.

nodes.name: Anglican Church in North America
nodes.blurb: Formed in 2009 by conservatives leaving the Episcopal Church, recognized by many global-South Anglican provinces.

nodes.name: Anabaptists
nodes.blurb: Zurich radicals who baptized one another as believing adults in 1525 — the 'radical reformation' of gathered, non-violent, suffering churches.

nodes.name: Hutterites
nodes.blurb: Anabaptists who hold all goods in common, after Acts 2 — still living communally on the North American plains.

nodes.name: Mennonites
nodes.blurb: Named for Menno Simons, the Dutch priest who gathered the scattered Anabaptists into enduring peaceful churches.

nodes.name: Amish
nodes.blurb: Jakob Ammann's stricter Mennonites — plain dress, plain living, and community set apart from the modern world.

nodes.name: Brethren (Schwarzenau)
nodes.blurb: German Pietists baptized in the river Eder in 1708, blending Anabaptist and Pietist streams — ancestors of the Church of the Brethren.

nodes.name: Quakers (Society of Friends)
nodes.blurb: George Fox's movement of the Inward Light — silent waiting on God, plain speech, and early witness against slavery.

nodes.name: Baptists
nodes.blurb: John Smyth's exiled English congregation embraced believers' baptism in Amsterdam in 1609; General (free-will) and Particular (Reformed) streams soon grew.

nodes.name: Particular Baptists
nodes.blurb: London's Calvinistic Baptists, whose 1689 confession still anchors Reformed Baptist churches — the stream of Bunyan, Carey and Spurgeon.

nodes.name: Southern Baptist Convention
nodes.blurb: Formed in 1845 in a split over slavery (a beginning it has formally repented of); today the largest Protestant denomination in the United States.

nodes.name: National Baptist Conventions
nodes.blurb: The great Black Baptist conventions, gathered in 1895 — the churches that carried, and led, the American civil-rights movement.

nodes.name: American Baptist Churches USA
nodes.blurb: The northern Baptist family, organized nationally in 1907 — the tradition of Roger Williams and the first Baptist church in America (1638).

nodes.name: Methodism
nodes.blurb: John Wesley's heart 'strangely warmed' in 1738; with Charles's hymns and field preaching, a revival society inside the Church of England became a worldwide church.

nodes.name: Methodist Episcopal Church
nodes.blurb: At the Christmas Conference in Baltimore, American Methodism became a church of its own — and grew faster than the frontier.

nodes.name: African Methodist Episcopal Church
nodes.blurb: Richard Allen, once enslaved, led Black Methodists out of segregated pews into the first Black-led denomination in America.

nodes.name: The Salvation Army
nodes.blurb: William and Catherine Booth took Wesley's gospel to London's poorest streets — soup, soap and salvation, under a flag instead of a steeple.

nodes.name: Holiness Movement
nodes.blurb: A revival of Wesley's teaching on entire sanctification — camp meetings and new denominations seeking a deeper work of grace. The dry tinder Pentecostalism would set alight.

nodes.name: Church of the Nazarene
nodes.blurb: The largest Holiness denomination, united at Pilot Point, Texas in 1908.

nodes.name: Christian & Missionary Alliance
nodes.blurb: A. B. Simpson's missionary movement around the 'fourfold gospel' — Christ our Saviour, Sanctifier, Healer and Coming King.

nodes.name: United Methodist Church
nodes.blurb: The 1968 union of American Methodism's main branches; in 2022 the Global Methodist Church separated from it over sexuality and Scripture.

nodes.name: Second Great Awakening
nodes.blurb: Camp-meeting revival swept the American frontier — filling the Methodist and Baptist churches and birthing the restorationist movements below.

nodes.name: Stone-Campbell Movement
nodes.blurb: 'Christians only, no creed but Christ' — Barton Stone's and the Campbells' movements united in 1832 to restore simple New Testament church life.

nodes.name: Churches of Christ
nodes.blurb: The a cappella stream of the restoration movement, congregational to the core.

nodes.name: Disciples of Christ
nodes.blurb: The restoration movement's ecumenical stream, known for weekly communion at an open table.

nodes.name: Plymouth Brethren
nodes.blurb: Believers in Dublin began breaking bread with no clergy at all; J. N. Darby's teaching here shaped dispensationalism and much of evangelical prophecy belief.

nodes.name: Millerites
nodes.blurb: William Miller's movement expected Christ's return in 1844; the Great Disappointment scattered them — into several adventist bodies.

nodes.name: Seventh-day Adventist Church
nodes.blurb: Organized in 1863 with Ellen White among its founders — Sabbath-keeping, health-minded, and now one of the most widespread Protestant bodies on earth.

nodes.name: Azusa Street Revival
nodes.blurb: Speaking in tongues at Topeka (1901) and then William Seymour's multiracial revival on Azusa Street (1906) — the spring from which world Pentecostalism flows.

nodes.name: Church of God in Christ
nodes.blurb: Bishop C. H. Mason's church embraced the Azusa revival in 1907 — the largest Pentecostal denomination in the United States.

nodes.name: Church of God (Cleveland, TN)
nodes.blurb: An Appalachian holiness fellowship (1886) that received Pentecost after Azusa — now a global movement.

nodes.name: Assemblies of God
nodes.blurb: Organized at Hot Springs, Arkansas in 1914; today the largest Pentecostal family in the world.

nodes.name: Foursquare Church
nodes.blurb: Aimee Semple McPherson's Angelus Temple and its worldwide offspring — Jesus the Saviour, Baptizer, Healer and Coming King.

nodes.name: Apostolic Faith Mission (South Africa)
nodes.blurb: Azusa's fire reached Johannesburg with John G. Lake in 1908 — South Africa's first Pentecostal church, and the AFM remains one of its largest.

nodes.name: Charismatic Movement
nodes.blurb: From 1960 the gifts of the Spirit crossed into the historic churches — Anglican, Catholic, Reformed — renewing worship far beyond Pentecostal denominations.

nodes.name: Calvary Chapel
nodes.blurb: Chuck Smith welcomed the barefoot Jesus People of the California coast — verse-by-verse teaching and casual worship that reshaped evangelicalism.

nodes.name: Vineyard Movement
nodes.blurb: John Wimber's 'signs and wonders' churches — intimate worship and everyday ministry in the Spirit's power; its songbook went round the world.

nodes.name: British New Church Movement
nodes.blurb: House churches seeking New Testament church life with present-day apostles and prophets — the stream of Terry Virgo's Newfrontiers and many church-planting families.

nodes.name: NCMI (New Covenant Ministries Int'l)
nodes.blurb: A South African-born apostolic team relating to local churches across the nations — part of the same restoration stream as Newfrontiers.

nodes.name: Four12 Global / Joshua Generation
nodes.blurb: Joshua Generation Church (Cape Town) grew into Four12 Global — a partnership of churches across several nations, contending for apostolic New Testament Christianity.

nodes.name: Non-denominational & Evangelical
nodes.blurb: Independent Bible-centred congregations — from megachurches to house fellowships — now one of the largest 'families' in world Christianity, though they'd resist the label.

nodes.name: African-Initiated Churches
nodes.blurb: Thousands of churches founded by African leaders — Ethiopian, Zionist and Apostolic streams — now numbering tens of millions across the continent.

nodes.name: Zion Christian Church
nodes.blurb: Engenas Lekganyane's church, headquartered at Moria in Limpopo — its Easter gathering is among the largest Christian assemblies anywhere.

nodes.name: Kimbanguist Church
nodes.blurb: Born from Simon Kimbangu's brief 1921 ministry in the Congo — he spent the rest of his life in a colonial prison; the church grew regardless.

nodes.name: Redeemed Christian Church of God
nodes.blurb: Founded in Lagos in 1952, now planting churches on every continent — a flagship of Nigeria's extraordinary missionary energy.

nodes.name: Unitarian Universalism
nodes.blurb: New England Congregationalism's liberal wing set aside the Trinity; merged with the Universalists in 1961 into a creedless association.

nodes.name: Latter-day Saints (Mormons)
nodes.blurb: Joseph Smith's restoration movement with new scripture (the Book of Mormon) and a distinct doctrine of God — outside Nicene Christianity by its own account and others'.

nodes.name: Christadelphians
nodes.blurb: John Thomas's Bible-study movement, rejecting the Trinity while awaiting Christ's literal kingdom.

nodes.name: Jehovah's Witnesses
nodes.blurb: Charles Taze Russell's Bible Students, renamed in 1931 — door-to-door witness, a distinct New World Translation, and a non-Trinitarian theology.

nodes.name: Christian Science
nodes.blurb: Mary Baker Eddy's teaching that sickness is an error of mind to be healed by prayer — a 19th-century 'metaphysical' reading of Christianity.

nodes.name: Oneness Pentecostalism
nodes.blurb: The 'New Issue' that split early Pentecostalism — baptism in Jesus' name only and a rejection of the Trinity; today's United Pentecostal Church and related bodies.
